topicArea: Night, the Conquest of Darkness & the Annexed Country

questions.passage:
The satellite photograph shows the planet after sundown, and the dark is losing. Cities burn as white webs, highways string them together, coastlines are drawn in light, and the blackness survives only in oceans, deserts, and the poorest places. Somewhere under that glow, a woman in her nineties can still describe the sky over her childhood farm, the whole spilled arch of the galaxy, visible on any clear night to anyone who looked up. The majority of people now alive have never seen it. Something was traded for the glow, and the terms of the trade were never really read aloud.

Before the conquest, night was not simply day's absence; it was a different country with its own customs. Households slept in two shifts, a first sleep and a second, with a waking hour between for prayer, talk, or quiet work, a rhythm so ordinary it needed no name until it vanished, surviving now only in court records, diaries, and the puzzled footnotes of historians who kept finding it. Curfew bells emptied the streets; the watch walked them; law, labor, and authority largely ended at dusk, and the dark that harbored dangers also harbored exemptions, a nightly commons where the day's arrangements loosened. Night had its own economy, its own sociability, and its own clock.

The annexation came with the lamps, gas first and then electric, and the vocabulary of the campaign was frankly territorial: night was opened up, pushed back, conquered. Lit streets extended the day's order into the dark hours, the factory discovered that machines need no sleep and invented the shift to match, and the city learned to run all night. Light served commerce and it served control, the illuminated street being easier to police than the dark one, and within three generations the different country had been absorbed into the empire of the day, its customs abolished, its border erased.

The ledger of the annexation is genuinely two-columned, and honest accounting reads both. The gains are not decoration: lit streets made evenings usable and safer, night hours multiplied work, study, and sociability, and populations long confined indoors by darkness, not least women, gained mobility that the dark had taxed. But the losses column is real and mostly unpriced. Darkness itself disappeared as an experience; the sky was erased city by city until the galaxy became a rumor; and bodies keyed for millions of years to the sun's schedule now run on the empire's, which never closes, a mismatch whose costs are paid nightly and read, when they are read at all, as personal failings rather than infrastructure.

The annexed night is also unevenly staffed. An entire economy runs while its beneficiaries sleep, warehouses, hospitals, kitchens, transit yards, and the staffing runs downhill: night work belongs disproportionately to those with the fewest alternatives, its documented wear concentrated where the wages are thinnest. The night shift is the annexation's bill, and it is delivered, as such bills usually are, to a different address than the benefits, and stamped paid by people who never saw the invoice.

A countermovement has formed around what remains. Dark-sky preserves now protect blackness the way earlier parks protected trees, travelers pay to stand under skies their grandparents got free, and darkness has completed the modern cycle by which a commons becomes a scarcity and then a commodity. The movement's demand deserves precise statement, because it is routinely caricatured: nobody proposes unlit streets or a restored medieval dusk. The demand is calibrated light, illumination aimed where it serves and shielded where it spills, brightness treated as a decision rather than a default, which is less a rebellion against the conquest than an audit of its excesses.

The grandmother's sky, then, is not merely a scenic loss. Night was a country, and it was annexed the way countries usually are, profitably, unevenly, and with the account books kept by the annexing side. The glow in the satellite photograph is the empire's flag. But annexations can be renegotiated at the margins, a light left burning is a decision that can be revisited, and the stars have the one advantage of all patient things: they are still there, above the glow, waiting, as they always have, on the reconsideration.
questions.stem: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The dark-sky movement's preservation project is ultimately doomed, since darkness has already completed the familiar cycle from open commons to priced commodity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Artificial light annexed the distinct country of night, with real gains and unpriced, unevenly billed losses; the standing question is calibration.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because lit streets gave confined populations mobility and safety, the conquest of night should be counted a straightforward triumph.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The disappearance of segmented sleep proves that pre-industrial night customs were superior to modern schedules.

explanations.A: This choice reads the commodification cycle as a verdict of futility. The sixth paragraph presents the preserves and the priced sky as what the countermovement formed around, then states its live demand, calibrated light, an audit of excesses, and the conclusion holds the renegotiation open, the stars waiting on reconsideration.
explanations.B: Correct. The passage establishes night as a different country with its own customs, narrates the frankly territorial annexation, reads both columns of the ledger, gains not decoration, losses real and mostly unpriced, shows the bill delivered downhill to the night shift, and lands on the movement's precise demand: brightness as a decision rather than a default, annexation renegotiable at the margins.
explanations.C: This choice keeps one column of a two-columned ledger. The gains are granted in exactly these terms, safety, mobility, multiplied hours, but the accounting is called honest only when both are read, and the erased sky, the mismatched bodies, and the downhill staffing fill the column this choice drops.
explanations.D: This choice converts an exhibit of difference into a ranking the passage never makes. Segmented sleep shows night had its own clock and customs; nothing declares the old rhythm superior, and the sixth paragraph explicitly disowns restored medieval dusk.

questions.stem: The primary function of the sixth paragraph is to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: concede that the dark-sky preserves inevitably commodify the very darkness they protect, quietly undermining the movement they were built to serve.

choices.label: B
choices.text: argue that municipalities should adopt shielded street lighting on a fixed schedule.

choices.label: C
choices.text: present the countermovement and rescue its demand from caricature: not unlit streets but calibrated light.

choices.label: D
choices.text: demonstrate that astronomy tourism has made darkness profitable enough to guarantee its preservation.

explanations.A: This choice converts an observation into an indictment the paragraph does not file. The commons-to-commodity cycle describes darkness's new scarcity value; the paragraph proceeds to state the movement's demand sympathetically and precisely, not to expose its preserves as self-defeating.
explanations.B: This choice hardens a stated principle into municipal policy. Illumination aimed where it serves and shielded where it spills characterizes the demand's content; no ordinance, schedule, or implementation program is proposed.
explanations.C: Correct. The paragraph introduces what formed around what remains, preserves, paying travelers, the completed cycle, then performs its central move: the demand deserves precise statement because it is routinely caricatured, nobody proposes unlit streets, the actual demand being calibrated light, brightness treated as a decision rather than a default, an audit of the conquest's excesses rather than a rebellion against it.
explanations.D: This choice mistakes an irony for a guarantee. Travelers paying for skies their grandparents got free marks the commodification, and nothing suggests the market secures darkness's future; the movement exists precisely because preservation requires demand-making, not because tourism settled it.

questions.stem: The description of night as a different country with its own customs, now annexed, most nearly conveys that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: national borders were historically easier to cross under the cover of darkness.

choices.label: B
choices.text: pre-industrial law formally designated the hours of darkness as foreign territory.

choices.label: C
choices.text: rural and agricultural regions retained their traditional night customs considerably longer than the rapidly electrifying cities did.

choices.label: D
choices.text: night once constituted a socially distinct domain, its own clock and loosened arrangements, which light absorbed into the day's order.

explanations.A: This choice literalizes the country into geography. The metaphor's borders are temporal, dusk as the frontier where the day's authority ended, and smuggling across national lines appears nowhere in the passage.
explanations.B: This choice mistakes a figure for a statute. No law designated darkness foreign; the second paragraph's curfew bells and watch mark the day's institutions withdrawing at dusk, the customs arising in the space their withdrawal left.
explanations.C: This choice converts the metaphor into a rural-urban timeline. The grandmother's farm sky illustrates the erased galaxy, not a surviving customs regime; the annexation is narrated as general, absorbed within three generations.
explanations.D: Correct. The second paragraph supplies the country's contents: two-shift sleep with its waking hour, the nightly commons where the day's arrangements loosened, its own economy, sociability, and clock. The third narrates the annexation in the campaign's own territorial vocabulary, opened up, conquered, lit streets extending the day's order until the different country was absorbed into the empire of the day, its customs abolished, its border erased.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the conquest of night is best described as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: celebratory, since the annexation freed confined populations and multiplied usable hours.

choices.label: B
choices.text: wholly condemning, treating the entire gains column as self-serving propaganda kept by the annexing side's own accountants.

choices.label: C
choices.text: nostalgic, urging a return to the curfew bells and two-shift sleep of the different country.

choices.label: D
choices.text: two-columned by design: gains granted as real, losses pressed as unpriced and unevenly billed, remedy framed as audit.

explanations.A: This choice keeps the ledger's first column and closes the book. The gains are not decoration, the passage grants, but the losses column is real and mostly unpriced, and the annexation's bill is delivered to a different address than the benefits, none of which celebration can absorb.
explanations.B: This choice discards the concessions the fourth paragraph makes unmistakably. Safety, mobility for the dark-taxed, multiplied hours: these are entered as genuine, and the account books kept by the annexing side describes the pricing of losses, not a fabrication of gains.
explanations.C: This choice attributes to the author the caricature the sixth paragraph disowns. Nobody proposes unlit streets or a restored medieval dusk, the passage insists; the old country's customs are described to establish what changed, not prescribed for restoration.
explanations.D: Correct. The fourth paragraph announces the method, the ledger is genuinely two-columned, and honest accounting reads both, and the passage executes it: real gains named without irony, real losses named as unpriced, the bill traced downhill, and the closing posture calibration and reconsideration, an audit of excesses rather than a rebellion against the conquest.

questions.stem: A suburb proposes floodlighting its parks all night for safety, with fixtures that scatter light broadly upward and outward. Based on the fourth and sixth paragraphs, the author would most likely respond that the proposal:

choices.label: A
choices.text: raises a genuine safety consideration but treats brightness as a default rather than a decision, the unshielded spill being the excess the audit targets.

choices.label: B
choices.text: should be rejected outright, since the passage establishes that lit public spaces serve institutional control rather than genuine safety.

choices.label: C
choices.text: vindicates the conquest entirely, since public parks are precisely the shared commons that darkness once taxed most heavily.

choices.label: D
choices.text: is unobjectionable so long as the suburb also funds a dark-sky preserve elsewhere.

explanations.A: Correct. The fourth paragraph enters safety among the gains that are not decoration, so the consideration is genuine; the sixth defines the standard the fixtures fail: illumination aimed where it serves and shielded where it spills, brightness treated as a decision rather than a default. All-night broadcast floodlighting is the default unexamined, the excess the audit targets, without the safety aim requiring it.
explanations.B: This choice sharpens a both-and into an either-or. Light served commerce and it served control, the third paragraph says, and lit streets made evenings safer, the fourth adds; the passage's framework prices the spill, it does not deny the safety.
explanations.C: This choice borrows the mobility gain to bless the method. The dark's tax on movement is a granted cost of the old country, but vindication of floodlighting does not follow; the calibration demand governs how the gain is delivered, aimed and shielded, not whether lighting may exist.
explanations.D: This choice invents an offset scheme the passage never contemplates. The preserves protect what remains; they are not indulgences purchasing spill elsewhere, and the demand is calibration at the fixture, not compensation at a distance.

questions.stem: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the second paragraph's claim that two-shift sleep was an ordinary, general rhythm of pre-industrial nights?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that the waking hour between the two sleeps was used quite differently across regions and eras, for prayer in some places and for quiet household work in others.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evidence that wealthy households, able to afford candles, sometimes stayed awake later than poor ones.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Re-analyses finding references to first and second sleep rare, concentrated in literary sources, and as consistent with occasional broken sleep as with a standard rhythm.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evidence that some modern sleepers, placed in artificial darkness for long periods, consolidate into single unbroken sleep.

explanations.A: This choice varies the interval's contents, which the claim itself already does, prayer, talk, or quiet work; regional differences in how the hour was spent presuppose the hour, confirming the rhythm's generality rather than testing it.
explanations.B: This choice documents class variation at the night's front edge, later bedtimes among the candle-rich, which is compatible with the two-shift pattern governing sleep once begun; the claim concerns the rhythm's structure, not the hour it started.
explanations.C: Correct. The claim rests on the pattern being so ordinary it needed no name, a generality inferred from documents. References revealed as rare, clustered in a few literary sources, and ambiguous between a standard rhythm and occasional broken sleep would dissolve the evidential base for ordinariness, leaving a vivid custom possibly never general at all.
explanations.D: This choice runs the wrong direction for the claim's content. Modern consolidation under artificial darkness bears on what bodies do now under experimental conditions; the claim is historical, about what pre-industrial households did, and present-day sleepers cannot testify to it either way.

questions.stem: Which of the following findings would most strengthen the fifth paragraph's claim that night work's costs are distributed downhill?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Labor data showing night shifts concentrated in the lowest wage quartiles, health wear falling on those workers, persisting where day positions were unavailable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evidence that some highly paid professionals, among them surgeons, air traffic controllers, and commercial pilots, also routinely work demanding overnight hours.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Surveys finding that most night workers, when asked directly, report preferring their current shifts to the alternative of unemployment.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evidence that cities with larger nighttime economies show higher overall employment rates.

explanations.A: Correct. The claim has three joints: night work belongs disproportionately to those with the fewest alternatives, its wear concentrates where wages are thinnest, and the staffing runs downhill rather than by preference. Concentration in the lowest quartiles supplies the first, health wear falling on those workers the second, and persistence where day work was unavailable rather than declined the third, the bill's address confirmed.
explanations.B: This choice offers exceptions the claim's disproportionately already accommodates. Surgeons and pilots at night show the shift is not exclusively low-wage, which the paragraph never asserted; the distribution, not the roster's purity, is the claim.
explanations.C: This choice compares night work to joblessness, a baseline the claim does not use. Preferring a shift to unemployment is consistent with having the fewest alternatives, indeed it is what having the fewest alternatives sounds like, so the survey supports the mechanism if anything.
explanations.D: This choice measures the annexed night's aggregate output, employment lifted by the 24-hour economy, which belongs to the ledger's gains column; who staffs those hours and who absorbs their wear, the downhill question, is left untouched.
